summary: Orbital/space compute becomes economically compelling once Starship is reusable, potentially cheaper than terrestrial compute on a per-gigawatt basis
summary_extended: Baker argues that orbital/space-based compute will become economically superior to terrestrial compute once SpaceX's Starship achieves full reusability, presenting specific cost math to support the claim. His analysis breaks down a terrestrial gigawatt data center at roughly $60B ($25B for power and cooling infrastructure plus $35B for IT equipment), then notes that space eliminates the power/cooling cost entirely, leaving only the $35B IT equipment cost plus launch costs. With a reusable Starship, Baker estimates launch costs at approximately $5B, yielding a total orbital gigawatt cost of ~$30B versus ~$60B terrestrially — a 2x cost advantage. He further argues that because terrestrial power and cooling costs are 'reasonably inflationary,' the economic gap between orbital and terrestrial compute will widen over time, making the thesis more compelling as Starship matures. The investment implication is speculative and long-term, contingent on Starship reusability being achieved, and SpaceX remains private so there is no directly actionable US-listed equity.
detail: Baker's math: terrestrial gigawatt costs ~$60B ($25B power/cooling + $35B IT equipment). In space, no power/cooling needed, so the right comp is $35B IT equipment vs. cost of launch. With reusable Starship, launch cost ~$5B, so total orbital gigawatt cost ~$30B vs. $60B terrestrial. Power/cooling costs are 'reasonably inflationary' making the gap widen over time.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents specific math but frames it as forward-looking and contingent on Starship reusability; described as his own analysis
quote_fragment: you can put a gig into space for 30 billion and a gig on earth is 60 billion

ticker_audit.reasoning: Virgin Galactic/Virgin Galactic Holdings is unrelated to Starship orbital compute; SpaceX is private and there is no US-listed equity that is a direct, unambiguous beneficiary of this specific thesis — SPCE is a different and irrelevant space company and should not be added.
